coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs/SBS composites and its application performance as asphalt modifier
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Pinhui
Song, Xiaoqing
Dong, Mingliang
Sun, Haiming
Wu, Wenxin
Zhang, Rui
Sun, Min
Zhao, Xingchi
coredata.dc:description: This research aims to use the prepared bio-base carbon quantum dots/styrene–butadiene styrene block copolymer (CQDs/SBS) composite materials as asphalt modifiers based on Pickering emulsion polymerization. The physical and chemical properties of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and its effect on asphalt modification were researched and contrasted it with SBS modifier. The results show that strong van der Waals forces and chemical covalent bonds are formed between CQDs and SBS polymer chains through a series of physicochemical reactions, resulting in strong interactions. Thus CQDs are uniformly dispersed in the SBS phase to form a homogeneous composite material. With the addition of CQDs, the CQDs/SBS composite modifier with slightly lower molecular weight than SBS modifier and can be better dispersed in asphalt. In addition, CQDs/SBS composite modifier has better thermal stability. Furthermore, CQDs and SBS have a synergistic impact in progressing the high-temperature property of polymer modified asphalt (PMA), which is showed by the improvement in softening point, viscosity, rutting resistance, and elastic recovery rate of CQDs/SBS PMA. However, the low-temperature property of CQDs/SBS PMA decreased, which is manifested by decreased penetration and ductility, increased creep stiffness modulus (S), and decreased the rate of change of stiffness with time (m). Further, CQDs/SBS PMA appears superior better storage stability than SBS PMA. It has the preferences of facile preparation procedure and low cost too, making it a beneficial choice for asphalt modifiers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
CQDs/SBS composite modifier
Modified asphalt
Rheological properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural analysis of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier
Fluorescence microscopy analysis of CQDs/SBS composite modifier
Function groups analysis of modifiers by FT-IR
Thermal stability analysis of modifiers by TG
Molecular weight distribution of modifiers by GPC
Frequency sweep of PMAs at medium and high temperature
Multiple stress creep recovery (MSCR) of PMAs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of experimental plan procedure of this research.
Manufacturing of CQDs/SBS composite modifier.
Fluorescence microscopy of CQDs/SBS composite modifier at (a) 200 px (b) 400 px.
FT-IR spectrum of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier.
TG and DTG analysis of (a) SBS modifier and (b) CQDs/SBS composite modifier.
GPC date of modifiers (a) CQDs/SBS composite modifier and (b) SBS modifier.
Viscosity-temperature properties of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA (a) viscosity of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at different temperature (b) Viscosity-temperature curve fitted by Sall model.
(a) Master curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at 30 °C and (b) change of shift factor of frequency sweep curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA to temperature.
(a) shift factor of frequency sweep curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA to temperature fitted by Arrhenius-like equation and (b) activation energy for CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA.
(a) G’ and (b) G” versus temperature of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA.
(a) G*/sinδ and (b) temperature with the G*/sinδ = 1.0 KPa for CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA.
MSCR (ten cycle) for CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA.
(a) R and (b) Jnr at 40 °C and two stress level for CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA.
S and m at different loading time for CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at (a) −18 °C and (b) −24 °C.
The ratio of S and m for CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at 18 °C and 24 °C.
Properties of base asphalt binder.
TG and DTG parameters of SBS modifier and CQDs/SBS composite modifier.
Molecular weight distribution data of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and the SBS modifier
Physical properties of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA

row.entry: Storage stability (48 h softening point difference)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formula and parameters of viscosity-temperature curve of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA fitted by Sall model.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs/SBS composites and its application performance as asphalt modifier

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Transportation Engineering, Shandong Jianzhu University, Jinan 250101, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Transportation Engineering, Shandong Jianzhu University, Jinan 250101, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yantai Highway Development Center, Yantai 265600, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yantai Highway Development Center, Yantai 265600, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong High Speed Group, Jinan 250098, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong High Speed Group, Jinan 250098, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research aims to use the prepared bio-base carbon quantum dots/styrene–butadiene styrene block copolymer (CQDs/SBS) composite materials as asphalt modifiers based on Pickering emulsion polymerization. The physical and chemical properties of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and its effect on asphalt modification were researched and contrasted it with SBS modifier. The results show that strong van der Waals forces and chemical covalent bonds are formed between CQDs and SBS polymer chains through a series of physicochemical reactions, resulting in strong interactions. Thus CQDs are uniformly dispersed in the SBS phase to form a homogeneous composite material. With the addition of CQDs, the CQDs/SBS composite modifier with slightly lower molecular weight than SBS modifier and can be better dispersed in asphalt. In addition, CQDs/SBS composite modifier has better thermal stability. Furthermore, CQDs and SBS have a synergistic impact in progressing the high-temperature property of polymer modified asphalt (PMA), which is showed by the improvement in softening point, viscosity, rutting resistance, and elastic recovery rate of CQDs/SBS PMA. However, the low-temperature property of CQDs/SBS PMA decreased, which is manifested by decreased penetration and ductility, increased creep stiffness modulus (S), and decreased the rate of change of stiffness with time (m). Further, CQDs/SBS PMA appears superior better storage stability than SBS PMA. It has the preferences of facile preparation procedure and low cost too, making it a beneficial choice for asphalt modifiers.

ce:section.ce:para: As the transportation develops, the issues of overloading and canalization of traffic are becoming increasingly more serious, and there are more issues, such as rutting, potholes, bulging, and cracking of asphalt pavement [1]. As such, the choice of materials is very important to promote the performance of asphalt pavement. The lack of viscoelastic properties of conventional asphalt binders (such as base asphalt) can easily lead to plastic deformation of asphalt pavements under high temperature and heavy traffic situations. However, polymer modified asphalt（PMA)is considered good to meet the high- and low-temperature performance of asphalt pavements. Among all the PMAs, styrene-butadiene copolymer (SBS) PMA has the benefits of both high- and low-temperature performance, and is gradually used as asphalt pavement materials. However, it also continues to have shortcomings, such as high initial cost, poor compatibility and poor storage stability [2].

In view of the shortcomings of SBS PMA as mentioned, many researchers have made research on composite PMA to get a more cost-efficient modification method. Nanomaterials have the characteristics of large specific surface area and small size, they show some novel properties and unusual characteristics in comparison to ordinary materials, making it possible to be used as additives for SBS PMA [3–5]. Polacco [6] and Sureshkumar [7] prepared nano-montmorillonite/SBS composite PMA and nano-montmorillonite/ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA) copolymer PMA respectively, to promote the rheological capabilities of the asphalt binder. Sun [4] compared the capability of 5 wt% SBS PMA with 0.5 wt% nano silica (NS) + 5 wt% polymerized styrene butadiene rubber (SBR) + 1 wt% polyethylene (PE) composite PMA, and got the result that the composite PMA had significantly improved at the high/low temperature and anti-aging performances. Yusoff’s research show that the water damage resistance and aging resistance performances of NS/SBS composite PMA have significantly improved [8]. Goli [9] showed that carbon nanotubes significantly improved the storage stability and rheological performances of SBS PMA. Xin [1] found that carbon nanotubes and polyethylene have a synergistic impact in promoting the high-temperature performance of asphalt (shown as an increase in modulus, viscosity, etc.), which is reflected in the improvement of rutting resistance. In addition, Zhang proposed three best SK-70 base asphalt nanoparticles/polymer PMA systems: 3 wt% nano-ZnO/0.5 wt% nano-TiO2/3.7 wt%SBS, 5 wt% nano-ZnO/4.2 wt%SBS, and 5 wt% nano-CaCO3/4 wt%SBR. The results show that the nanomaterials are well scattered in the asphalt, which can promote the scatter of the polymer in the asphalt, promote the compatibility of the polymer and the asphalt, and promote the extreme temperature performance of the base asphalt [10].

Research on nanomaterials and their derivatives show that the application of nano-modifiers in asphalt is generally very promising. Within this scope, this research aims to explore the application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as asphalt modifiers. CQDs are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with extremely small particle sizes (generally approximately 2–5 nm) and unique fluorescent properties [11–14]. Studies show that the carbon element in the asphalt accounts for more than 80% and the average size of the asphaltene core is approximately 2 nm [15]. According to a similar compatibility principle, there should be very good compatibility between CQDs and asphalt binder. In addition, compared with other nanomaterials, CQDs have certain acidity and surface activity due to the large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces. This has the potential to cause some physical or chemical reaction between CQDs and the unsaturated dilute bonds of SBS, resulting in the formation of stable chemical crosslinks that effectively play a synergistic role in both. Therefore, CQDs are expected to be a useful choice for nanomaterials/SBS composite PMA. However, there is no report about CQDs as an asphalt modifier.

In addition, the current processes for preparing nanomaterial/SBS composite PMA mainly include mechanical blending, melt blending, solution blending, and in-situ polymerization [16,17]. Among them, the first three methods are the most widely used due to their simple operation, but there are problems such as uneven dispersion of nanoparticles in the polymer modifier and inclination of agglomeration and segregation. In-situ polymerization refers to fully mix nanomaterials, polymer monomers, and catalysts to initiate polymerization to obtain nanomaterials/SBS composites. Although this method can improve the dispersion of nanomaterials, the reaction conditions are difficult to control. Therefore, to address the above problems of nanomaterials/SBS composite PMA, it is also urgent to develop a new arrangement procedure of nanomaterial/SBS composite PMA to improve the dispersion of nanomaterials in SBS and the combination between them, and to better play the role of composite modification. The Pickering emulsion polymerization method is expected to become a new choice for preparing of nanomaterials/SBS composite PMA. The so-called Pickering emulsion refers to the emulsion obtained by using ultra-fine solid particles as emulsifier. Studies show that CQDs with suitable surface structure can be used as nano-emulsifying agents to get ready or stabilize Pickering emulsions [18–20]. The polymer Pickering emulsion can be further polymerized to obtain nanoparticle/polymer composites with good dispersion, tight bonding, and uniform texture. In my previous research, I selected low-value-added biomass pyrolysis residues as molecular precursors, and prepared high-quality bio-based CQDs with a simple one-step pyrolysis method at 400 °C. This method is cost-effective and suitable for large-scale preparation. In addition, the prepared CQDs show prevalent surface action, and the styrene/water Pickering emulsion arranged as a nanoparticle surfactant shows very high steadiness [18].

Based on the above discussion, the purpose of this article is to prepare a novel CQDs/SBS composite PMA and elaborate the procedure of its preparation. In order to achieve this goal, this thesis first uses biomass-based CQDs and SBS as the main raw materials, and adopts the Pickering emulsion polymerization method to develop a new type of CQDs/SBS composite material as a modifier of asphalt for the first time. Secondly, a series of chemical analysis instruments were used to compare the functional group structure, molecular weight, and thermal stability of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and pure SBS modifier. Based on this, the conventional physical performances and rheological performances of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA were compared. Finally, the cost-effectiveness of CQDs/SBS composite modifier is analyzed. In general, this work will provide a modifier with excellent performance for nano-material/SBS composite PMA, which has certain significance for popularizing the application of nano-material/SBS composite PMA. Fig. 1 shows a graphical flow diagram of the experiments in this research.

SBS PMA and CQDs/SBS PMA was prepared with base asphalt (60/80 penetration grade), bio-based CQDs [18], and linear SBS modifier as raw materials, among which the last two were used as raw materials to prepare CQDs/SBS composite modifier for the first time. Table 1 shows the physical properties and polar four-component composition of the base asphalt. In addition, bio-based CQDs were prepared according to the method reported by the author [18]. Basically, the biomass residues, thermally polymerized at 400 °C for 2 h under the protection of a high-purity nitrogen atmosphere, generated the bio-based CQDs after purification.

The CQDs/SBS composite modifier was made by Picking emulsion polymerization method and the preparation process is shown in Fig. 2 . Initially, 3.0 g CQDs were dispersed into 60 g pure water and ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min. Then, 60 g of SBS modifier toluene solution (the mass percentage of SBS modifier is 20 wt%) is added to the CQDs dispersion. Then, a Bitumen Modified & Emulsified (BME) type shearing machine was used to prepare Pickering emulsion at a high speed of 3000 r/min for 5 min. Next, the aforementioned Pickering emulsion was poured into a clean glass flat-bottom tray. Finally, the tray was set in a vacuum drying stove at 100 °C for 12 h. Under this condition, self-polymerization occurred and Pickering emulsion finally converted into CQDs/SBS composite modifier. As shown in Fig. 2, the CQDs/SBS composite modifier represents a yellow solid at room temperature. In this process some series of physical and chemical reactions occurred between CQDs and SBS. Since the surface of CQDs contains a large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, while SBS, as a block copolymer, has a large relative molecular weight and contains a large number of unsaturated double bonds. On the one hand, very strong van der Waals forces are generated between CQDs and SBS, and the larger the relative molecular weight is, the larger the van der Waals forces are. On the other hand, due to the special preparation conditions, the carboxyl (COOH) can release hydrogen ion（H+)in aqueous solution, which produces positively charged carbenium ions after combining with the double bonds of SBS as a catalyst. And the highly reactive SBS carbenium ions can undergo nucleophilic addition reactions with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CQDs, thus linking SBS and CQDs. Although the efficiency of the above reaction is not high, due to the large molecular weight of SBS and the large number of double bonds contained in a single molecule and the large number of carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups contained on the surface of CQDs, CQDs can be used as a chemical cross-linking point to cross-link SBS under certain conditions. After the above reaction process, a stable CQDs/SBS composite modifier with van der Waals forces and covalent bonding crosslinking is formed. In addition, the increase in oxygen content can improve the polarity of the CQDs/SBS composite modifier, thereby increasing its compatibility with asphalt [22]. The other functional groups in the CQDs/SBS composite modifier show similar structures to those in the SBS modifier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier

ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic phase and dispersion state of CQDs in SBS modifier were characterize with a fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX51, Japan). The sample is stimulated under blue light and the swollen SBS phase emits green fluorescence, which can be captured by a fluorescence magnifying lens prepared with a DP72 computerized camera. Then, the Pickering emulsion was dropped between two glass sheets which were in a vacuum drying stove at 100 °C for 12 h. After the above processes, the emulsion was polymerized into a thin film between the two glass sheets and the sample is observed with the Cellsens of a fluorescence microscope.

We used FT-IR to identify the chemical functional groups of modifiers and to analyze their chemical composition [21]. The experiment uses the Thermo Science Nicolet IS 5 infrared spectrometer, the scanning recurrence is 32 times per minute and wave number is from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1.

GPC (PL-gel) prepared with a differential refractive index finder was utilized to decide the atomic weight of the modifiers. 1, 2, and 4-trichlorobenzene were taken as the dissolvable. At that point the average molecular weight and dispersion index (PDI) was connected to portray the atomic weight dissemination of modifiers to supply the atomic data of materials.

Thermogravimetric (TG) 209 F3 Nevio thermogravimetric analyzer was used for the thermal analysis of the modifier. In a nitrogen atmosphere, approximately 7 mg of sample was heated from 30 °C to 600 °C at a steady heating rate of 10 °C /min. The nitrogen stream rate was 120 ml/min during the experiment. Further, TG and DTG (Differential thermogravimetric) bends were connected to assess the thermal stability of the modifier. In order to ensure repeatability, all the experiments were performed three times.

This research prepared CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA by utilizing the melt mixing strategy. The liquid asphalt was weighed and poured into a cylindrical stainless steel barrel and then further heated to 180 °C, and 4 wt% of the asphalt mass modifier and 3 wt% of the compatibilizer were added, which were then high-speed sheared at 4000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the temperature was adjusted to 170 °C, and the high-speed shearing was changed to slow stirring (800 r/min). After 0.25 wt% stabilizer was added, the mixture was stirred for 3 h to obtain a uniform PMA sample.
Referring to the Chinese standard “Standard Test Method for Asphalt and Asphalt Mixture for Highway Engineering” (JTG E 20-2011), we carried out the physical properties of PMA.

A controlled stress dynamic shear rheometer (DSR, Anton Paar Smart Pave 102) can characterize the rheological performances of CQDs/SBS composite PMA and SBS PMA samples. The scanning results of isothermal frequency (0.1–50 mads) at 30, 45, 60, and 75 °C were obtained. The temperature scan range was 58 °C to 95 °C with a heating rate of 1 °C/min and a frequency of 10 rad/s. Multi-stress repeated creep tests were carried out at 100 Pa and 3200 Pa, and each stress cycle was 10 cycles, where each cycle was subjected to 1 s stress loading and 9 s relaxation.

The bending beam rheometer (BBR) can measure the creep properties of PMA at very cold weather, while the asphalt at this temperature is too hard to be tested on DSR. Two indicators can be obtained from the BBR, namely the creep stiffness S and the change rate m value of the stiffness changing with time. In order to avoid the cracking of asphalt at low temperatures, the PG classification specification requires that the S of the BBR loaded for 60 s is not more than 300 MPa and the m value is not less than 0.3. The temperature of the BBR test was −18 °C and −24 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence microscopy analysis of CQDs/SBS composite modifier
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence micrographs of the CQDs/SBS composite modifier at 200 Px and 400 Px are shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3, the green part represents the SBS modifier phase, which is formed by the SBS particles swelling after absorbing toluene. Although CQDs can also emit fluorescence, it is very weak under the selected blue excitation light source [18], showing an opaque dark black. Fig. 3 shows that the CQDs phase is uniformly dispersed in the SBS modifier phase in irregular flakes, and there is no obvious aggregation. It shows that CQDs disperses well in SBS modifier, which is conducive to the development of composite modification. In addition, the homogeneous dispersion of CQDs in SBS also indicates the existence of sufficient interfacial interactions between CQDs and SBS polymer matrix, which may result from the formation of strong van der Waals forces and chemical cross-linking between CQDs and SBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Function groups analysis of modifiers by FT-IR
ce:section.ce:para: The position and intensity of infrared absorption peaks reflect the structural characteristics of molecules and can be used to identify the structural composition of polymer or to determine their chemical moieties. The infrared spectroscopy of CQDs/SBS and SBS modifiers are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4 shows that the infrared absorption peak of the CQDs/SBS composite modifier is comparable to that of the SBS modifier, especially within the fingerprint group region (1330 cm−1–400 cm−1) [10]. The sharp peaks around 2924 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational absorption peaks of CH2 and CH3 on the greasy chain. There are numerous retention crests between 3100 and 3000 cm−1, which are the extending vibration retention crests of unsaturated hydrocarbons. Moreover, the simultaneous absorption peaks near 1640 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, 1582 cm−1, and 1492 cm−1 are due to stretching vibrations of the CC bond. Their simultaneous appearance reflects the presence of a benzene ring. The 1280–1030 cm−1 region includes CO stretching vibration and CC single bond skeleton vibration. The sharp peak around 966 cm−1 is due to the torsional vibration of the CC bond, while the absorption peak around 911 cm−1 is due to the surface swaying vibration of CH2. The retention crests around 699 cm−1, 722 cm−1, and 747 cm−1 are caused by the vibrational absorption of mono-substituted benzene. It is worth noting that the sharp absorption peaks at 966 cm−1 and 699 cm−1 belong to the characteristic absorption peaks of the SBS modifier. Based on the infrared spectroscopy of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier, we can know that CQDs/SBS composite modifier, contrasted with the SBS modifier, contains a broad peak at 3100–3700 cm−1, which is the extending vibration peak of OH. At the same time, there's a self-evident bear crest near1760 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1, which represents the CO absorption peak of the carboxyl group. It is worth noting that, the infrared spectrum of the modifier shows that the CQDs/SBS composite modifier contains more oxygen- functional groups (including CO, OH and CO) compared with the SBS modifier, which primarily come from CQDs[18]. However, the absorption peak of OH stretching vibration in CQDs/SBS is weaker and wider compared to CQDs, while the CO stretching vibration is shifted to lower wave number (from 1570 cm−1 in CQDs to 1550 cm−1 in CQDs/SBS). The above phenomenon is caused by the nucleophilic addition reaction between the SBS carbenium ions and the carboxyl or hydroxyl group to the nucleophilic addition reaction, which results in the reduction of the OH content and the formation of additional hydrogen bonds. At the same time, the reaction formed a P-π conjugated system of RCH2 CH2 CH+ R, which made the CO stretching vibration to lower wave number. It is noteworthy that the chemical linkage between CQDs and SBS formed stable covalent bonding in the CQDs/SBS composites by the surface of the FTIR analysis, which is sufficient to circumvent the possible phase separation that can easily happen in fabrication of nanocomposites via physical mixing of polymer and CQDs. Moreover, the increment in oxygen substance can promote the extremity of the CQDs/SBS composite modifier, subsequently expanding its compatibility with base asphalt [22]. In addition, the other characteristic absorption peaks in the CQDs/SBS composite modifier have similar frameworks to those in the SBS modifier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability analysis of modifiers by TG
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability of the modifier is a critical feature we can think about when analyzing the composition structural characteristics of the PMA [23]. Herein, the thermogravimetric strategy was used to characterize the stability, composition and thermal decomposition processes of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier. As shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2 , although both the modifiers have experienced two major weight-loss stages, their thermodynamic behaviors were rather different. During the first stage, the weight-loss occurred from 30 °C to 350 °C. The loss of the modifier was mainly caused by the volatilization of the crystal water adsorbed on the sample surface and the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups (OH, COOH) contained in the molecule. As the surface of CQDs contained more oxygen-containing functional groups, the weight-loss rate of CQDs/SBS composite modifier is obviously higher than that of SBS modifier in the first stage. The weight-loss in the second stage occurred in the temperature range of 350 to 500 °C, and the loss of the modifier is mainly caused by the decomposition of SBS modifier to form small molecules and volatilization [23,24]. This was the primary weight-loss period as illustrated by the emotional occasion in the DTG bend for each folio. We can know from the TG curve that the pyrolysis initiation temperature of CQDs/SBS composite modifier is 9.1 °C lower than that of the SBS modifier, which is caused by the further decomposition of oxygen-containing useful bunches on the surface of CQDs. However, the pyrolysis termination temperature of the two modifiers is approximately 479 °C with limited differences. Meanwhile, when the weight-loss rate was the largest (DTG peak), the DTG curve shows that the pyrolysis temperature corresponding to CQDs/SBS composite modifier was 3 °C higher than that of SBS modifier, and the weight-loss rate was 6.76%/min less than that of SBS modifier. The residual mass of CQDs/SBS composite modifier was 15.17% after the pyrolysis rate and the mass change was 84.80%. while the residual mass of the SBS modifier was only 0.43%, and the mass change was 99.55%. This shows that SBS can be almost completely decomposed into small molecules and volatilized without generating residual carbon in this temperature range. However, the CQDs/SBS composite modifier contained CQDs with carbon core as the main structural unit. After this part of CQDs lost the oxygen- functional groups in the first weight-loss stage, the remaining carbon core had very good thermal stability and it took longer for decomposition, so the CQDs/SBS composite modifier had a higher residual quality. The above analysis shows that CQDs/SBS composite modifier has better thermal stability in comparison to SBS modifier, representing that the expansion of CQDs through the incorporation of chemical cross-linking reactions with SBS moves forward the thermal stability of the SBS modifier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular weight distribution of modifiers by GPC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 and Table 3 display the GPC chromatogram and molecular weight distribution of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier. Fig. 6 shows that the frames and positions of the peaks of the two modifiers are very semblable. There is a main peak near 5.38 and a shoulder peak at the attachment of 5.25. This is led by the inconsistent degree of polymerization of SBS, and CQDs did not affect the degree of aggregation of SBS subjects. Simultaneously, the molecular weight and PDI value of the CQDs/SBS composite modifier are smaller than those of the SBS modifier, but the difference between them is very small. In addition, the chemical cross-linking of CQDs with SBS is equivalent to replacing part of the SBS, which results in a decrease in the molecular weight of CQDs/SBS.

Table 4 shows the physical properties of PMA prepared with CQDs/SBS and SBS as modifiers. As shown in Table 4, the penetration and ductility of CQDs/SBS PMA are diminished in contrast with SBS PMA, and the softening point, 60 °C vacuum dynamic viscosity and elastic recovery increased. Therefore, the addition of CQDs improves the high-temperature performance of PMA, whereas the low-temperature performance diminishes. Moreover, the storage stability of CQDs/SBS PMA is essentially superior than that of SBS PMA. Recent studies have noticed that there is an interface transition zone between the SBS modifier stage and the base asphalt stage, and put forward the theoretical hypothesis of the interface steadiness of SBS PMA, which declares that the stability of the interface is closely relavant to the modification effect of SBS [25,26]. CQDs nanosheets by chemical cross-linking tend to adsorb on the SBS/asphalt interface, helping in the improvement of the solidness of the interface, subsequently expanding the storage stability of the PMA.

In addition, the temperature will affect the high-temperature flow characteristics of PMA, and the flow characteristics of different samples are sensitive to changes in temperature, that is, the temperature affectability of asphalt is different, which can be affected by the high- and low-temperature zone. The temperature sensitivity in the high-temperature zone is tightly relevant to the construction workability characteristics of the PMA asphalt mixture and the pumping of the PMA, which is one of the basic contents of the rheology research of asphalt flowing at high-temperature. Thus, in this paper the viscosity of the PMA at high-temperature (over 100 °C) was studied using rotational viscometer (Brookfield type). Fig. 7 a shows that the viscosity of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA decreases rapidly and then stabilizes with an increase in temperature. In the same test temperature, the viscosity of CQDs/SBS PMA is higher than that of SBS PMA. It is suggested that chemical cross-linking between CQDs and SBS increases the polymer strength. However,the difference between them becomes smaller as the temperature increases.

The Saal model proposed by ASTM D2493 (Eq. (5)) is further used to process the viscosity-temperature curve to feature the temperature affectability of CQDs/SBS and SBS PMA.
(5)
lg
lg η ∗ 1000
= n + m · lg
T + 273.13

In the above formula, m represents the slope of the regression line; n represents the intercept of the regression line on the lg (lgη*1000) axis; η represents the viscosity Pa·s; and T represents the temperature °C. The Saal fitting curve of the viscosity-temperature curve of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA is shown in Fig. 7, and the Saal model estimators obtained are shown in Table 5 . In addition, m in the Saal model is generally defined as the viscosity temperature index (VTS). The absolute value of VTS is directly proportional to the change of viscosity with temperature and better is the temperature sensitivity.
Fig. 7b and Table 5 show that the absolute value of VTS of SBS PMA is lower than that of CQDs/SBS PMA, indicating that the addition of CQDs adversely affects the temperature sensitivity of SBS PMA.

The performance of the asphalt pavement generally relies on the viscoelastic properties of the PMA. The linear viscoelasticity of PMA is very sensitive to the movement and interaction of polymer molecular chains. Meanwhile, the complexity of different high molecular polymer modification systems also leads to complicated influences on the inside frame of PMA. However, the rheological parameters in the linear viscoelastic scope are only related to the properties of the material itself, instead of depending on changes in stress and strain. Therefore, linear viscoelasticity is successful to clarify the impact of the polymer modifiers on the performance of PMA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frequency sweep of PMAs at medium and high temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 displays the master curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at 30 °C. This curve is obtained by shifting the frequency sweep curves at 30 °C, 45 °C, 60 °C, and 75 °C. Fig. 8 indicates that the complex shear modulus G* of CQDs/SBS PMA is continuously more prominent than that of SBS PMA at the same frequency, appearing that the addition of CQDs can improve the high-temperature quality and elasticity of SBS PMA. Moreover, the master curve of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA in the low ω zone is quite diverse, but the difference decreases as the recurrence increments. Concurring to the guideline of time–temperature equivalence, the low-frequency zone corresponds to the high-temperature zone. Hence, CQDs/SBS PMA has superior high-temperature performance, implying that the addition of CQDs improves the rutting resistance of SBS PMA. The G* value of the two PMA tests in the high-frequency zone is nearly the same, demonstrating that CQDs have very little effect on the viscoelastic performance of the SBS PMA in the high-frequency zone.

It can be observed from the master curve in Fig. 8a that the principle of time–temperature equivalence can be well applied to CQDs/SBS and SBS PMA. The change of shift factor of frequency sweep curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA to temperature is shown in Fig. 8b. The shift factors of frequency sweep curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at high-temperatures are rather different. In this paper, the Arrhenius-like equation is used to fit the alter of shift factor of frequency sweep curves of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA to temperature as shown in Fig. 9 a. The differences between shift factors of frequency sweep curves of different PMA can be quantitatively recognized by the activation energy according to the Arrhenius equation [27]. Furthermore, the activation energy can be used to characterize the temperature sensitivity of the PMA. As shown in Fig. 9b, the activation energy of CQDs/SBS PMA is more noteworthy than that of SBS PMA, showing that greater energy needs to be overcome to cause asphalt deformation. It further appears that the CQDs by chemical cross-linking promotes the high-temperature performance of SBS PMA.

In addition to the master curve, this paper also conducted the temperature sweep of the sample in a wide temperature range of 58 °C –95 °C. Fig. 10 displays the storage modulus G′ and loss modulus G″ changing with temperature. G′ and G″ decrease drastically with the temperature rises, whereas the decreasing rate of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA is different. CQDs/SBS PMA has a higher rate of decrease of G′ and G″ than SBS PMA with increasing temperature. This further shows that CQDs/SBS PMA has poorer temperature sensitivity than SBS PMA.

Moreover, the rutting factor G*/sinδand the test temperature corresponding to G*/sinδ = 1.0 Kpa (failure temperature) are used to characterize the rutting resistance of the PMA [28]. It is generally considered that the asphalt is sufficient to resist rutting when G*/sinδ is not less than 1.0 kPa, and we can calculate the failure temperature based on the critical point. Fig. 11 a shows that the rutting component G*/sinδ of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA changing with temperature is in accordance with the changes of G′ and G″. The corresponding temperatures of SBS and CQDs/SBS PMA at G*/sinδ = 1.0 KPa are 74.63 and 74.94 respectively (Fig. 11b), implying that the rutting resistance of SBS PMA is worse than that of CQDs/SBS PMA. Further, the G*/sin δ of CQDs/SBS PMA is slightly higher than that of SBS PMA in the scope of 58–76 °C. When the temperature exceeds 76 °C, the difference between them becomes smaller.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple stress creep recovery (MSCR) of PMAs
ce:section.ce:para: A large number of studies have shown that the conventional test indexes such as softening point, 60 °C dynamic viscosity and rutting factor are very limited to evaluate the high-temperature performance of PMA. In recent years, in order to accurately evaluate the high temperature performance of PMA, many scholars have proposed MSCR test index for evaluating the high temperature stability of PMA, and the reliability of the evaluation index has been verified [28,29]. Generally, MSCR indicates loading the sample for 10 cycles, with each cycle loading the stress for 1 s, and then removing the stress and recovering for 9 s. In this paper, MSCR is performed under two stress levels (100 Pa and 3200 Pa) at 40 °C, and the following two parameters are obtained: recovery rate R and irrecoverable compliance Jnr, which are calculated by recoverable and irrecoverable strains, respectively.

The strain responses of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA at 40 °C and two stress levels (100 Pa and 3200 Pa) for 10 cycles are shown in Fig. 12 . Considering one cycle, the strain at the conclusion of the crawl stage of CQDs/SBS PMA and the strain at the conclusion of the recuperation stage are both littler than that of SBS PMA. To quantitatively compare the high-temperature performance of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA, the R and Jnr of the two samples at 40 °C and two stress levels (100 Pa and 3200 Pa) are recorded in Fig. 13 . Under the same experimental test parameters, the R of CQDs/SBS PMA is more noteworthy than that of SBS PMA, whereas Jnr is less than that of SBS PMA. It appears that the expansion of CQDs increments the high-temperature rutting resistance of SBS PMA. Moreover, the increment of stress increases the R of SBS PMA, whereas diminishes the R of CQDs/CQDs PMA marginally. The Jnr of both the PMAs decreases. It shows that they both have good anti-heavy load recovery ability.

The main damage form of asphalt pavement under low-temperature conditions is low-temperature cracking as the asphalt mixture shrinks and deforms when the temperature of the pavement decreases. The cohesive force between the asphalt mixture layer and the lower layer prevents the movement caused by this shrinkage, and tensile stress is generated in the asphalt binder. Cracks occur when this tensile stress is greater than the tensile strength of the asphalt mixture. As such, it is required that asphalt have a faster creep rate to relax the stress generated at low temperatures or in the process of cooling.

BBR is used to estimate the creep properties of PMA at exceptionally low temperatures. It employments the beam principle to characterize the cracking tendency of PMA when the temperature decreases. Two indicators can be obtained from BBR, namely creep stiffness modulus (S) and the rate of change of stiffness with time (m), which are used to characterize the ability to resist load and to relax stress at low temperatures, respectively. If S is too large, it indicates that cracks are more likely to occur. A lower m value means that the relaxation capacity is insufficient to dissipate the stress caused by the temperature drop and it will increase the probability of cracks.

Fig. 14 shows the changes with time in S and m of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA measured by BBR at −18 °C and −24 °C. We can know that S declines rapidly with the extension of the loading time, while m increases significantly. However, the S and m of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA and their rates of changes are different. When the loading time is the same, the S of CQDs/SBS PMA is larger than that of SBS PMA, whereas m is smaller. This shows that the addition of CQDs increases the brittleness of SBS PMA and deteriorates its low-temperature crack resistance. CQDs comprise of disordered graphite carbon with strong hardness. Therefore, the addition of CQDs increases the strength, hardness, and brittleness of SBS PMA. In addition, the S and m of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA increase with a decrease in temperature, indicating that CQDs contribute more to the brittleness of SBS PMA at very low temperatures.

Lower S and higher m imply that PMA has a stronger resistance to splitting at low temperatures. Thus, in this research, the S/m ratio at 60 s was utilized to feature the low-temperature cracking resistance of PMA. The S/m is inversely proportional to the low-temperature performance of asphalt. Fig. 15 shows that the lower the temperature is, the greater is the S/m ratio and the worse is the anti-cracking ability. In addition, the S/m of CQDs/SBS PMA is higher than that of SBS PMA at the same temperature, implying that the low-temperature crack resistance of CQDs/SBS PMA is poor.

The CQDs used in this study are the waste (generally called biomass residue) remaining in the biomass pyrolysis process as raw materials prepared by simple thermal polymerization. The biomass residue is of very low price and the preparation process of CQDs is very simple, with high yield. As such, it is suitable for large-scale preparation. The above reasons make the price of CQDs very low, much lower than that of SBS. Therefore, CQDs are used instead of part of SBS (approximately 15%) in the CQDs/SBS composite modifier to significantly reduce the cost of the modifier. In addition, the use of Pickering emulsion polymerization to prepare CQDs/SBS composite modifier has the advantages of simple preparation process, uniform dispersion of CQDs in SBS, and excellent performance. Therefore, CQDs/SBS is a low-cost, high-performance composite modifier, and a beneficial choice for asphalt binder material modifiers.

In this paper, the Pickering emulsion polymerization method was utilized to prepare the CQDs/SBS composite modifier, and the asphalt was modified. The physical and chemical properties of CQDs/SBS composite modifier were characterized. Furthermore, the physical and rheological properties of CQDs/SBS composite PMA and SBS PMA were studied. According to the results and discussion, we can draw the following conclusion:
(1)
The CQDs/SBS composite modifier was prepared by a simple Pickering emulsion polymerization method. The strong van der Waals forces and chemical cross-linking between CQDs and the SBS polymer matrix enable the CQDs modifier can be consistently scattered in the SBS modifier to form a homogeneous composite material.
(2)
The physical and chemical properties of CQDs/SBS composite modifier and SBS modifier were contrasted. The outcomes appear that chemical linkage between CQDs and SBS formed stable covalent bonding in the CQDs/SBS composites. This make the CQDs/SBS composite modifier contains more oxygen-containing functional groups than the SBS modifier. The molecular weight of CQDs/SBS composite modifier is somewhat lower than that of SBS modifier. Moreover, the pyrolysis rate of CQDs/SBS is lower than that of SBS, and the residual mass is higher than that of SBS, showing better thermal stability.
(3)
The conventional physical properties and rheological properties of CQDs/SBS PMA and SBS PMA were compared. Due to the strong chemical covalent bond between CQDs and SBS, under the same content of modifier, softening point, viscosity, complex shear modulus, activation energy, rutting factor, and recovery rate of CQDs/SBS PMA are higher than those of SBS PMA, appearing superior high-temperature performance. In any case, the penetration, ductility, and m of CQDs/SBS PMA is less than that of SBS PMA, and the S is more noteworthy than SBS PMA, showing the low-temperature performance of CQDs/SBS PMA gets to be worse. Moreover, the thermal storage stability of CQDs/SBS PMA is superior than that of SBS PMA.
(4)
The cost-effectiveness and preparation process of CQDs/SBS composite modifier were analyzed. CQDs/SBS composite modifier, as a type of composite modifier with simple preparation process, low-cost, and high-performance, has become a beneficial choice for asphalt binder material modifier.

The information used to bolster the discoveries of this research are from prior detailed researches cited before. This original copy does not include distributed Figures, Tables, and Charts before, so all Figures, Tables, and Charts of this original copy are unique.

This study was monetarily bolstered by the National Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (No. ZR2021ME189), Project of science and technology support for youth entrepreneurship in Colleges and universities of Shandong Province (2019KJG004).

Pinhui Zhao: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoqing Song: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Mingliang Dong: Conceptualization, Investigation. Haiming Sun: Data curation. Wenxin Wu: Data curation. Rui Zhang: Data curation. Min Sun: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xingchi Zhao: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to recognize numerous co-workers, students, and research facility associates for giving specialized offer assistance on instrument examination. Much appreciation to Bhakti and Jeanette, NativeEE Company for etymological counsel.
